nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, heavily-vegetated marginal zones in still or slow-flowing freshwater and brackish systems; ponds, lakes, backwaters, quiet stream pools, ditches, and coastal drainages

decorPreferences: dense surface and marginal planting (hornwort, water sprite, floating plants) for fry refuge
open swimming lanes near the surface
fine-leaf plants to break lines of sight between males
minimal flow — still or very gentle filtration preferred

diet.notes: Mosquitofish are opportunistic predators that readily accept high-quality micro-pellets, flake food, and frozen or live small invertebrates such as brine shrimp, daphnia, and mosquito larvae. In the wild, they feed on zooplankton, insect larvae, small beetles, mayfly nymphs, and detritus. Despite the common name, mosquito larvae make up a relatively small proportion of their natural diet. They will consume the fry of their own and other species.

goodWith: own species in a species-only setup with heavy planting
robust fast-moving surface fish of similar size in large, well-planted tanks
avoidWith: long-finned or slow-moving fish (notorious fin nipper)
small or juvenile fish (predation and harassment)
any species unable to defend against persistent fin-nipping

imageCandidates.notes: Female specimen showing typical body form and gravid spot. Clear image, good species ID value.
FishBase species image; confirm license before use.

summary: The Mosquitofish (Gambusia affinis) is a small but notably aggressive livebearer native to the southern United States and Mexico, introduced worldwide as a biocontrol agent against mosquito larvae. It is listed by the IUCN among the 100 World's Worst Invasive Alien Species and is seldom kept in the aquarium hobby.
careNotes:
Mosquitofish are seldom recommended as aquarium fish. They tend to be relentless fin-nippers and will consume the fry of other species, making them a poor choice for community tanks. A species-only setup with dense planting is strongly advised. Males (up to 4 cm) are notably smaller than females (up to 7 cm); keeping multiple females per male helps distribute harassment. The species tolerates a remarkably wide temperature range (12–29 °C), making it one of the more cold-hardy livebearers available, and can briefly tolerate near-freezing temperatures. It also tolerates brackish and low-oxygen conditions. Filtration flow should be gentle — the fish naturally inhabit still or slow water. Despite the name, mosquito larvae form only a minor part of the natural diet.

Conservation note: Gambusia affinis is listed by the IUCN Invasive Species Specialist Group as one of the "100 World's Worst Invasive Alien Species." It has been introduced to every inhabited continent for malaria and West Nile virus mosquito biocontrol and is now considered invasive outside its native range in the southern United States and northern Mexico, where it has been documented causing significant harm to native fish, amphibian, and invertebrate populations through predation and competition. In the EU, import, breeding, transport, and release are prohibited. In Australia it is classified as a noxious pest. Aquarists must never release specimens into natural waterways. Mosquitofish are easily confused with the Eastern Mosquitofish (Gambusia holbrooki), a closely related Atlantic-slope species; G. affinis is native to drainages west of the Appalachians. Many commercially sold "mosquitofish" may in fact be G. holbrooki.
breedingNotes: Mosquitofish are highly prolific livebearers, and breeding is considered nearly impossible to prevent when both sexes are present. Gestation lasts 16–28 days, with shorter periods at warmer temperatures, and each brood typically yields 10–100 fry. A single female can produce several hundred fry over the course of a year. Adults provide no parental care and will readily consume their own offspring, so dense floating and fine-leaf planting substantially improves fry survival. Males mature in 43–62 days; females born early in the season can mature in as little as 21–28 days. Males possess a gonopodium — a modified anal fin used for internal fertilisation — and females are able to store sperm to produce multiple broods from a single mating.

primary.notes: Primary husbandry source. Provided adult size (male 4 cm, female 7.5 cm), temperature 18–27°C, pH 6.0–8.0, hardness 5–30°H, minimum tank 30×20×20 cm (~12.5 L for a pair), diet, temperament, and breeding notes. Noted highly aggressive, notorious fin nipper, unsuitable for community tanks.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Gambusia affinis
additional.notes: Confirmed IUCN '100 World's Worst Invasive Alien Species' listing, EU prohibition on import/breeding/transport/release, Australia noxious-pest status, temperature extremes (survives up to 42°C briefly, stops growing below 10°C), gestation 16–28 days, male maturity 43–62 days, female 21–28 days, fry count ~60 per brood, wild lifespan under 1 year typical with captive max ~3 years. Diet: zooplankton, beetles, mayflies, caddisflies, invertebrates.

additional.name: IUCN Global Invasive Species Database — Gambusia affinis
additional.notes: Confirmed listing among 100 World's Worst Invasive Alien Species; global introduction history for malaria biocontrol; documented impacts on native fish, amphibians, and invertebrates.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding is considered virtually impossible to prevent when both sexes are present. Gestation lasts 16–28 days, producing 10–100 fry per brood, and adults provide no parental care. Dense planting is required for fry survival as adults will cannibalise freely. Females can store sperm and produce multiple broods from a single mating.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: IUCN-listed among the 100 World’s Worst Invasive Alien Species; import/release banned in the EU, Australia, and other jurisdictions.
